name: Phase 9 — The Black Ledger Gate & Lower Route Fork

text.content:
The Black Ledger Gate — Design Principles

The Black Ledger Gate is the lower exit of the First Under-Road Waystation. It is the obstacle between the party and the rest of the under-road. It is the second great threshold in this campaign — the Permission Door above was civic; this one is procedural.

The design question

The Permission Door asked: do you have paperwork?

The Black Ledger Gate asks: what are you below?

The gate does not check names. It does not check identities. It checks route categories. Cargo. Witness. Escort. Recovered. Authorized. Substitute. The party must declare a category — verbally, through tokens, through marks, through escort authority, or by force — and the gate responds to the declaration.

Tone for the table

This is bureaucratic, not magical. The gate does not speak. It does not threaten. It opens or refuses based on what is placed in its slots and what is said into its listening niches. The horror is that this works. Someone, long ago, formalised the procedure for being allowed beneath the world. The procedure still runs. It has not stopped running for centuries.

The category logic

The gate recognises seven categories of valid passage:

  1. Witnessed transfer — you and your asset are declared via the Witness Tally and a token.
  2. Escort authority — a route courier (Kethren) opens it for you.
  3. Warden authorization — the local warden (Savax) authorizes the passage.
  4. Forged tally — a clever paperwork forgery declares your category falsely but plausibly.
  5. False category — you talk your way into a category through roleplay and skill.
  6. Force — you break the gate. Costly but possible.
  7. Bypass — you go around (the cistern slit).

The party needs only ONE of these. Multiple will work; combinations are sometimes useful (Witness+Escort gives a cleaner transit). Some methods have side effects that matter in Phase 10.

What the gate does not do

  • It does not require any single PC's presence.
  • It does not require any single piece of evidence.
  • It does not prevent failure. Failed methods cost time, attention, and clocks — but the gate is not a dead end. The party will get through. The question is what they pay.
  • It does not reveal what is below it. The wayfinding marks at the Lower Route Fork point in three directions; the gate itself does not narrate.

What this gate carries forward

The method used affects Phase 10's opening:

  • Witnessed transfer: Phase 10 opens with the route's wardens treating the party as expected arrivals at the next station.
  • Escort authority: Kethren's leverage continues. Phase 10 opens with him as a present complication.
  • Warden authorization (Savax cooperative): Phase 10 opens with the route's wardens informed of the party as “corrected” cargo, with mixed implications.
  • Forged tally: Phase 10 opens with route-cleaners hunting for a paperwork irregularity.
  • False category: Phase 10 opens with the party operating under a self-assigned category that may or may not hold up.
  • Force: Phase 10 opens with active pursuit.
  • Bypass: Phase 10 opens with the route oblivious to the party's passage — for now.

Player-Facing Description (First Approach)

The corridor ends at a gate.

The gate is not built like the Permission Door above. The Permission Door was a slab — an honest, mason-built panel of pale stone with a single inscription, a token socket, and a tally row at its base. It looked like what it was: an old toll point made permanent in stone.

This is not that.

This gate is darker stone, smaller in proportion to the corridor, and worked with more care. It is set into the wall flush, with no visible joint. It has no handle — not on your side. The face of it is divided into panels by shallow vertical and horizontal ridges, like a wall has been laid out as a grid for record-keeping. Each panel has a small recess. The recesses are different sizes.

There are token grooves at chest height, three of them in a row, sized for the discs you have seen elsewhere in the station. There is a witness tally cut in the lower-left corner, a small carved hollow shaped to hold a wax impression or a tally fragment. There are three shallow marker slots across the upper face of the gate, where flat plates can be set.

An inscription runs along the top of the gate in route shorthand. It has been partly scraped — not destroyed; deliberately scraped, the way a clerk underlines an instruction that should not be confused with the surrounding text. What remains is brief.

Unwitnessed cargo waits. Witnessed passage continues.

There is no other instruction.

The gate does not glow. It does not hum. It is not warm. It is simply waiting.

What the party can examine

  • The token grooves — three slots, sized for arrival, witness, and escort tokens respectively.
  • The witness tally cut — a small carved hollow where the Witness Tally Fragment from the Account Hall fits.
  • The three marker slots — high on the face of the gate. Each slot can hold a small stone or wax marker declaring a category.
  • The inscription — the scraped shorthand. A History DC 14 or Insight DC 13 confirms: the inscription is not a password. It is a statement of policy. The gate has just told you what it is, and what it does.

What is happening around the gate

Depending on the play state:

  • If Savax is alive and at his post: a route signal is running through the brass channel above the gate, monitored from the Warden Post.
  • If the route-cleaners are still active: the Burn Furnace may still be feeding. The Account Hall behind the party may be in active sweep.
  • If the Black Ledger Gate's mechanism has already been activated for another transit in the last hour, the marker slots may have residual marks from the previous passage — useful intelligence about who came through, and useful raw material for a forged-tally opening.

Method A — The Witness Method

The cleanest opening. The party declares a witnessed transfer using the Witness Tally Fragment and the appropriate tokens.

Required materials

  • The Witness Tally Fragment (Handout 2; recovered at the Witness Registry Wall).
  • One witness token (from the Toll-Token Cage or the Warden Post).
  • One additional token declaring the transfer kind: arrival, escort, or recovery.

Procedure

  1. Place the Witness Tally Fragment in the witness tally cut at the gate's lower-left corner.
  2. Place a witness token in the leftmost token groove.
  3. Place the second token (arrival, escort, or recovery) in the middle token groove.
  4. Speak aloud the route declaration: “Witnessed transfer; [your category] passage continues.”

The gate's mechanism registers the declaration. A soft click sounds. The recess on the inside of the gate releases. The gate slides upward into the lintel over the course of about ten seconds, smoothly and silently.

Consequences

  • Alert Clock: 0 advance.
  • Record Destruction Clock: 0 advance.
  • Breath and Stone Clock: 0 advance.
  • Phase 10 opens cleanly. The next waystation expects the party as ordinary route arrivals.

What the party should bring out of the waystation to use this

  • Witness Tally Fragment (Account Hall / Witness Registry Wall).
  • One witness token (Toll-Token Cage shelf or Warden Post box).
  • One transfer token (arrival = a Tarnsmere descent token if they have one; recovery = the Warden Post recovery token; escort = if Kethren is with them, or an escort token from the cage).

If the party has the Recovery token from the Warden Post

This pairs especially well with a witness token. The party can declare themselves as “escort with one recovered asset” (the recovered asset can be Haskur if he's with them; can be the Unclaimed Witness if she's with them; can be an unspecified asset they claim is in their custody and the gate doesn't check very hard).

If Merrit Osk (the Unclaimed Witness) is with them and consents to be declared the witness, this is morally cleaner than declaring her as cargo. This is one of the moments where the party's treatment of Merrit matters.

If Kethren is with the party and his courier mark is in their hands

His mark counts as escort authority for this method, which is technically Method B (Escort Authority). Combined with witness tokens, it makes Method A even cleaner.

Method B — The Escort Method (Kethren)

When this method is available

If Kethren is alive, present, and cooperating — whether genuinely turned, leveraged with surface evidence, or in custody and bargaining for safety. He has the standing to open the Black Ledger Gate on his own authority.

Procedure

  1. Kethren approaches the gate. He places his gloved hand on the witness tally cut briefly — a gesture of identification, not authentication.
  2. He presses his bone fingerstamp (the thin vertical stroke crossed at three-quarter height) into the leftmost marker slot. The slot recognises the mark immediately.
  3. He speaks the escort declaration: “Escort and party; passage continues under route channel.”
  4. He places a small route token at the centre groove. This token is his personal courier authorisation, kept on his person, distinguishable from common escort tokens by an additional notch on the rim.

The gate opens. It slides upward in about eight seconds — slightly faster than Method A. The route signal sent through the brass channel confirms the passage to the wardens at the next waystation downward, who will be expecting Kethren's party.

Consequences

  • Alert Clock: 0 advance.
  • Record Destruction Clock: 0 advance.
  • Breath and Stone Clock: 0 advance.
  • Kethren's leverage grows. He has now used route authority on the party's behalf at two gates (the Permission Door above and now this). He has a clear, credible threat: “I have escorted you twice. The route recognises us as travelling together. If you turn on me, I report you to the next warden and they will receive you as cargo.”
  • The next waystation expects the party. Phase 10 opens with the route's wardens at the next station having been informed of Kethren plus a party of N. They will not be surprised. They will be polite, and they will have prepared.

If Kethren is captured but uncooperative

Method B is unavailable through threats alone. Kethren is willing to die rather than open the gate under coercion — or, more accurately, he is willing to make the party think he is, until he sees a better deal. Bargaining DC 18 (Persuasion or Deception). The party would need to offer him something concrete: safe passage back to a surface place of his choice, written immunity, a contact in Greyhawk City who will hide him, etc.

If Kethren is dead

Method B is unavailable. The party cannot use his mark without his hand and voice. The mark itself can serve as evidence later but does not open the gate.

What Kethren's player should know

If a PC is roleplaying Kethren under a captured-and-cooperating premise (not a standard PC, but possible in some campaigns), the GM should note: Kethren does not want to be seen using his escort authority on the party. He will use it only if he believes his survival depends on it, and he will resent the party for putting him in that position. Method B is the cleanest opening for the party and the most expensive for Kethren.

Method C — The Warden Method (Savax)

When this method is available

If Route Warden Savax is alive and either:

  • Persuaded that the party's category can be legitimately classified for transit (Persuasion DC 18 with valid argument; e.g. they present themselves as “surface witnesses to a corrupted recovery”);
  • Deceived into authorising them (Deception DC 18, harder because Savax is procedural by nature; require a plausible category claim plus tokens to support it);
  • Captured alive and willing to bargain. Savax's bargain is steep: he wants the party to leave the waystation intact and to allow him to file the day's events in a way that does not implicate him personally. He will not authorise a transit that is obviously hostile to the route's interests — he is a true believer in the procedure, not in the conspiracy's mission.

Procedure when Savax is cooperating

  1. Savax operates the route bell with a specific cadence (seven strikes, paused, seven strikes, paused, three strikes).
  2. He places his warden token (a token marked with a wide horizontal stroke and three notches) in the leftmost marker slot.
  3. He speaks the warden authorization: “Warden authorising transit. Party of N; category, [whatever the negotiation concluded].”
  4. The gate's mechanism queries the brass channel for confirmation. If the warden's authority is intact (it is, in most cases), the gate opens.

Procedure when Savax is captured and bargaining

Same procedure, but Savax wants something in return. Reasonable bargain points:

  • The party leaves the waystation by the same route, ensuring he is not the only warden alive to be blamed.
  • The party promises not to surface his identity to Caelith or to Iren.
  • The party leaves him a clean record — they do not damage the registry wall, the Burn Furnace, or the gate itself.
  • The party agrees to be classified as “corrected surface trespassers permitted lower transit” rather than as anything more glamorous. This is a specific category that exists in route language; it implies they were intercepted and processed.

Consequences

  • Alert Clock: 0 advance.
  • Record Destruction Clock: 0 advance.
  • Breath and Stone Clock: 0 advance.
  • The party is classified. Whatever category Savax authorised becomes their route identity. Phase 10's next waystation receives them under that category. If they are “corrected trespassers,” the next wardens may be wary of them. If they are “witnesses to a recovery,” the next wardens may want to interview them.
  • Savax survives. This matters in Phase 10 if the party returns to surface; Savax can be a future complication or asset.

What Savax knows (and may share)

If the party bargains with Savax in good faith, he can offer concrete information in addition to opening the gate:

  • Haskur (Andren) was processed through here as recoverable cargo, escorted by Kethren, and moved to the next waystation — he will not name it, but he will draw a route mark on a wax plate.
  • Kethren's escort authority extends two waystations further down the route. Beyond that, he loses standing too.
  • V. Korre's surface channel was closed on direct instruction from the channel beyond the Black Ledger Gate. Savax does not know who specifically. He knows the route mark of the deeper authority — the arc with three notches — but he will not draw it for the party.
  • The next waystation is approximately a day's walk down the route. The walk is not difficult but it is unsettling.

Insight DC 15 on Savax: he is telling the truth about what he knows. He is omitting things he knows but is not authorised to share.

Method D — The Forged Tally Method

When this method is useful

The party doesn't have a cooperative Kethren or Savax, and doesn't have a clean Witness Tally Fragment, but does have time, tools, and a few raw materials. They can forge a passage declaration.

Required materials

  • One blank token (from the Toll-Token Cage) repaired or cast (Smith's / Tinker's Tools DC 15 to repair the damaged lower-route token; DC 17 to cast a new one from a blank using the mold).
  • One wax impression of a route mark from elsewhere in the waystation — preferably from the Witness Registry Wall.
  • One stone marker plate — can be improvised from a tally plate pulled from the Account Hall walls (advance Record Destruction Clock by 1 if done loudly), or scavenged from the Witness Registry Wall's loose chips.

Procedure

  1. The party assembles a plausible category declaration. The most workable forgery is “substitute witness with escort token” — they declare themselves as the witness for a transfer whose original witness was lost or recalled.
  2. Place the repaired/cast token in the centre token groove.
  3. Place the wax impression in the witness tally cut.
  4. Place the marker plate (with the “substitute witness” mark scratched into it) in one of the three upper marker slots.
  5. Speak the substitute witness declaration: “Substituting; previous witness pending recall; transit continues.”

The gate's mechanism does not have a strong check against this forgery. It is mechanical and procedural; it reads the marks and tokens as documents, and it does not have a way to verify that the documents are real. The forgery works.

Consequences

  • Alert Clock: +0 or +1. If the forgery was assembled quietly (Stealth across the assembly), no advance. If assembled with noise (Smith's tools clamour, mason work in the Account Hall), +1.
  • Record Destruction Clock: +0 or +1. If a tally plate was pulled from the wall to make a marker, +1.
  • Breath and Stone Clock: +0.
  • Phase 10 starts with route-cleaners hunting for an irregularity. The forgery passes the gate, but it leaves traces. The next waystation's wardens will eventually read the declaration record and notice something off — a substitute witness with no upstream record of who was being substituted. The route audits. Slowly. Routinely. Phase 10 begins with the party knowing (from environment cues) that the route is reviewing their passage and may have figured out the forgery within a day or two.

Required checks

CheckDCYields
Smith's / Tinker's Tools15Repairs the damaged lower-route token.
Smith's / Tinker's Tools17Casts a new token from a blank.
Forgery Kit / Sleight of Hand15Scratches a plausible “substitute witness” mark into a stone plate.
Investigation13Identifies which Witness Registry mark to wax-impress (the one for substitute witness; History DC 16 isolates the variant).
History15Composes a plausible substitute witness declaration in route shorthand.

Method E — The False Category Method (Roleplay)

When this method matters

The party doesn't have the right tokens, doesn't have a cooperating Kethren or Savax, and doesn't want to forge. They have themselves, what they have observed, and the route's own language. They can declare themselves as one of the route's accepted categories — provided they can sell the declaration with confidence and minor evidence.

This is Caelith's foreshadowing line from his pre-descent note: “You can change the category by speaking the right words.”

Valid false categories

The gate will accept a verbal category declaration if it is:

  • Plausible for the party's apparent state (you cannot declare yourself the route's deepest authority without supporting marks; you can plausibly declare yourself a surface witness arriving for a scheduled transfer).
  • Supported by at least one tangible element — a token, a route mark, a piece of correspondence (a Korre channel slip, a route-cleaner's instructions, an Aerdy seal).
  • Delivered in route language. Speaking surface words at the gate works less well than speaking the route's shorthand declarations.

Useful category claims

1. Surface witness

“Surface witness arriving for scheduled tally; cargo waits above.”

Persuasion DC 15. Requires the party to have at least one surface token or surface paperwork (Korre's papers from Phase 8, an Aerdy commercial document). Works because the gate has a category for surface-side witnesses descending to confirm a transfer; this is plausible for an unaccompanied party.

2. Recovered asset and escort

“Escort and recovered asset; previous escort departed; transit continues.”

Deception DC 16. Requires the party to identify one of their own as the “recovered asset” (the asset does not have to actually be recovered; the route just reads the declaration). Works because the gate has a category for completing a transfer whose escort has changed.

3. Correction cargo

“Corrected surface trespassers permitted lower transit under provisional warden authorisation.”

Deception DC 17. Requires the party to display some token of having been processed (any token they took from the Toll-Token Cage works). Works because the gate has a category for surface trespassers who have been re-classified as transit-permitted — a category the route uses when wardens decide not to kill an intruder.

4. Substitute witness (without token)

“Substituting for recalled witness; transit per warden instruction.”

Deception DC 18. Hard, because the gate's mechanism prefers a token here, but the route does occasionally substitute without one. Works if the party can produce a piece of route paperwork suggesting their substitution — the Warden Instruction Strip's reference to substitute witnesses, for example.

The roll structure

One PC makes the principal social check (Persuasion or Deception per the category claim). Up to two other PCs can Help — one supplying a token or paperwork, one supplying corroborating gesture or knowledge. Each successful Help gives the principal +2 to the roll, or advantage if both Helps are strong.

Consequences

  • Alert Clock: +0 to +1, depending on how cleanly the declaration goes. A clean DC 17+ pass: no advance. A barely-passed DC 15: +1.
  • Record Destruction Clock: +0.
  • Breath and Stone Clock: +0.
  • Phase 10 starts with the party operating under their declared category. Whatever they said becomes their route identity for the duration of the next waystation's processing. This can be useful (treats them as expected) or complicated (commits them to a story that has to hold up under scrutiny).

What this method rewards

Players who paid attention to the route's vocabulary throughout Phase 9. The gate is not testing identity or merit; it is testing whether the party can speak the route's language. PCs who never bothered to read tally marks or registry entries are at disadvantage on the social roll. PCs who took the time to translate phrases earlier in the phase are at advantage.

GM nudge

If the table is struggling with how to claim a category, you can offer (in-fiction): a PC remembers a phrase from the Account Hall they didn't act on at the time. “Wait, didn't the Burn Furnace slip say something about substituting?” Give them the prompt. The category exists; they just need to commit to it.

Method F — The Force Method

When this is the right call

The party has run out of time. Clocks are advancing. Reinforcements are coming. They need to be on the other side of this gate in the next minute. They are willing to break the gate to get there.

What forcing the gate means

The Black Ledger Gate is harder to force than the Permission Door above. It is smaller stone but better-fitted, and the mechanism is more intricate. Three options:

  • Lever it from below using a crowbar, prybar, or thieves' tools rigged as a wedge — Strength (Athletics) DC 24, with Help from up to three other PCs. Repeatable, each attempt advances Alert and Breath and Stone clocks.
  • Break the gate's counter-chain by pulverising the wall to the right of the gate to expose the mechanism — Mason's tools DC 17, or improvised tools with disadvantage. Loud (advances Alert Clock +1 per minute of work), takes 10–15 minutes.
  • Magical force. Knock on this gate has stronger effects than on the Permission Door — the route system is more sensitive to magical intrusion. Knock opens one counter but causes a Backlash (see below). Disintegrate works on the visible face of the gate but reveals the mechanism behind, which is more complex than the slab; the party would still need to disable the mechanism after.

The Backlash hazard

Forcing the gate triggers a stone shock: the gate's mechanism is designed to fail loud rather than silent. When forced:

  • All creatures within 15 feet of the gate make a Constitution save DC 14 or take 4d8 thunder damage, half on save. The sound also carries through the brass channel to the Warden Post and (if Savax is alive) wakes any sleeping or distracted route personnel.
  • The Breath and Stone Clock advances by 2. Old supports throughout the waystation creak audibly.
  • The Alert Clock advances by 2. Wardens at the next waystation receive an emergency route signal.

The Tally Sentinel

Forcing the gate also wakes a construct — the Tally Sentinel — that has stood dormant in a wall niche of the antechamber on the far side of the gate since the gate was last forced (centuries ago). The sentinel is a route construct, roughly humanoid, four feet tall, stone-and-iron body assembled from waystation mason work. It does not speak. It does not attack first. Its function is to witness the forced opening — to physically observe the party crossing and record them as a procedural irregularity.

If the party engages it: use the Stone-Witness stat block from Phase 8, adjusted upward (HP 52, AC 17, +1 to attacks). The sentinel is more dangerous than the Phase 8 stone-witness; it is built specifically for unauthorised gate breaches.

If the party leaves the sentinel alone, it follows them through the gate at a slow walking pace, observing without engaging. It will accompany them to the Lower Route Fork and stop there. The party can outpace it but cannot easily lose it within the waystation.

Consequences

  • Alert Clock: +2.
  • Record Destruction Clock: +0 (the route does not need to destroy records if it has the party as an active intrusion to deal with).
  • Breath and Stone Clock: +2.
  • The gate is partly broken. It opens, but the mechanism is damaged. Closing it from the far side is not possible. Anyone coming after the party will see exactly what happened.
  • The Tally Sentinel is awake. Even if the party defeats or evades it, the route will know within hours that an unauthorised forced passage occurred. Phase 10 begins under pursuit.
  • Carry-forward: the party is marked. The route's deeper waystations will have received an emergency signal; the next station's wardens will be prepared with non-negotiable force.

When to use this method anyway

If the party's surface allies are dying, if Caelith is in active danger, if the Blooming Rot operation is days from a critical phase that the party needs to disrupt, this is the right method. Time is the cost. The party knows they have spent it.

Method G — The Bypass Method (Cistern Slit)

When this method is available

The party has located the Cistern Slit (Location 10) and identified that it runs past the Black Ledger Gate's foundation. They can crawl along it to emerge in the antechamber on the far side, then either continue on the lower route or open the gate from inside.

What the bypass requires

  1. Enter the slit from an interior access point (Account Hall south, Sleeping Alcoves corridor, or the maintenance bridge near the back of the chamber). Standard Athletics DC 14 to drop into the water.
  2. Travel east-southeast along the slit toward the gate's foundation. About 80 feet from the easternmost interior access to the gate's far side.
  3. Negotiate two narrow air-pocket sections. Each is about 15 feet long; the slit's ceiling dips and only the water flows through. PCs must hold their breath. Athletics DC 14 per section, or two breath-holds with Constitution check DC 13 each.
  4. Emerge in the gate's antechamber. The slit's east-end grate (the tampered one; see Location 10) opens with Investigation DC 13 from the inside, and admits a Medium creature without removed gear, or a Medium creature with removed gear and reasonable stealth.

What's on the far side

The gate's antechamber is small (about 12 feet square), dimly lit, and currently empty — unless the route-cleaners have anticipated a bypass attempt, in which case a Silent Courier is posted here. Perception DC 16 (the courier is good) to spot before being spotted.

From the antechamber, the party can either:

  • Continue without opening the gate. The Lower Route Fork is just beyond. This is the cleanest bypass — the route's wardens never learn that anyone went through.
  • Open the gate from inside. The gate's mechanism has an internal release lever. This requires Investigation DC 13 to locate. Opening from inside makes a soft click; the wardens may notice. Useful if the party wants the rest of their group to come through, or if they want to retrieve equipment from the waystation side.

Consequences

  • Alert Clock: 0 advance if no one was spotted in the antechamber; +1 if the Silent Courier engaged the party.
  • Record Destruction Clock: 0 advance.
  • Breath and Stone Clock: +1 only if the Cistern Pulse hazard fires during transit (it fires at Breath and Stone Clock 3+).
  • The gate remains formally sealed. The route does not know the party crossed. This is the most invisible method.
  • Phase 10 begins with the route oblivious. The next waystation is operating at normal vigilance. The party arrives unannounced and unexpected — useful, but also lonely; the route does not welcome them, and they have no procedural identity to fall back on.

Cost

  • Cold and exertion (Constitution checks during the swim).
  • Possible poison exposure if the swamp-gas pocket has reformed in the slit's middle (Constitution save DC 12 or 1 level of exhaustion). The Cistern Slit's gas was a Phase 8 hazard; it may be active in Phase 9 if the party advanced the Breath and Stone Clock above.
  • Equipment may be soaked. Wet route paperwork is degraded. The party must protect their handouts during the swim (Survival DC 13, or wrap them in waxed cloth from the Supply Cache).

What this method rewards

Caution and thoroughness. A party that explored the Cistern Slit early, identified the tampered east-end grate, and gathered the proper gear (waxed cloth, swimming preparation) has a real bypass option. This is especially valuable for parties who want to leave no trace at the gate.

Failure Handling at the Gate

Core principle

The Black Ledger Gate does not dead-end the adventure. Failure has costs — clocks, attention, alarm, hazard — but the party will get through. The question is what they pay.

What counts as a failure

  • Method A–C: speaking the wrong declaration aloud, placing a wrong token, or attempting an authorisation that the gate's mechanism rejects.
  • Method D: a forged element is poorly assembled or contradictory.
  • Method E: a roleplay check fails the DC for the chosen category.
  • Method F: the party stops short of full force; the gate doesn't budge.
  • Method G: a perception or athletics check fails during the cistern crawl.

What happens on failure

Mild failure (within 4 of the DC)

  • The gate does not open this time.
  • One token or marker may be locked into its slot — the party can't reuse it on the next attempt without retrieving it (a separate action).
  • Alert Clock: +0. The route does not know yet.
  • The party can try again with adjustment. Encourage them.

Serious failure (5 or more below the DC, or two consecutive mild failures)

  • The gate's mechanism registers an irregularity. A route signal sounds in the Account Hall and the Warden Post.
  • Alert Clock: +1.
  • If Savax is alive and not currently engaged elsewhere, he begins to move toward the gate.
  • One marker slot locks; that method is unavailable for the rest of this attempt.

Catastrophic failure (10 or more below the DC, or a serious failure in a method already weakened)

  • The gate's Backlash hazard fires: 2d8 thunder damage to all within 15 feet (Constitution save DC 14 for half). Less damaging than the full Force-method Backlash; this is the gate signalling refusal, not the gate breaking.
  • Alert Clock: +2.
  • Breath and Stone Clock: +1.
  • If the Tally Sentinel was sleeping, it stirs but does not yet activate. A second catastrophic failure wakes it.

What the gate does not do on failure

  • It does not kill the party. There is no “you fail the gate and die” outcome.
  • It does not seal forever. Even a catastrophic failure is recoverable — the party can fall back, regroup, and try a different method.
  • It does not refuse all methods. If two methods fail, the others are still available (with cost adjustment).

GM intervention guidance

If the table is getting frustrated:

  1. Re-read the gate inscription aloud. Sometimes players forget the gate is asking “what are you below?”. The inscription is the hint.
  2. Cue a category they haven't tried. “You remember the Sleeping Alcoves' panic prayer. You've been thinking about it. The prayer mentioned ‘not being witnessed.’ Could that be a category?”
  3. Offer Method G if the party hasn't found the Cistern Slit. A PC remembers there was a maintenance bridge that crossed dark water. They could go back and check it.
  4. Let a PC have an insight. If a player is genuinely stuck, give them a History or Investigation check at advantage to recall something from the Account Hall they didn't act on at the time.

What the gate is teaching

The gate is teaching the players that the under-road has rules, and rules can be played. It is not arbitrary. It is not cruel. It is procedural in a way that rewards understanding and punishes carelessness. The party should leave the gate scene with the conviction that they can navigate the route — if they read carefully. This conviction matters in Phase 10.

Location 14 — The Lower Route Fork

The chamber immediately beyond the Black Ledger Gate. About 20 feet square. Three corridors lead away from it — the route branches here. This is the Phase 9 end-state choice point.

What the party sees

You have crossed the gate. The corridor beyond opens almost at once into a small chamber, perhaps twenty feet square, with a ceiling slightly higher than the corridor — nine feet, maybe ten. The chamber is dim. There are no lamps here. Whoever uses this place either brings their own light or does not need any.

Three corridors lead away. One to your left, one straight ahead, one to your right. Each corridor begins with a route mark cut into the lintel above its entrance. You have seen these marks before — in the Under-Road Marker Gallery, in the branch motif you read on the gallery wall.

The left-hand corridor's lintel bears the recovery sign, the same sign that marked Haskur's registry entry.

The straight-ahead corridor's lintel bears Kethren's courier mark, paired with the seven-cuts variant.

The right-hand corridor's lintel bears the cargo-tally mark, with seed-and-wax indicators.

The chamber's floor is bare dressed stone. The air, if anything, is colder here than in the waystation. There is no sound at all. The cistern, which ran along the waystation's south edge, is here below your feet somewhere — you can feel the faintest vibration of slow water through the stone — but you do not hear it.

You are at the first true branching of the under-road.

The three routes

Left — The Recovery Route (Haskur Transfer Route)

Marked by the recovery sign. Boot marks on the floor at the corridor's entrance suggest several recent passages. The boots are mixed: at least two distinct sets. One narrow, careful, consistent with Kethren-style courier footwear; one heavier, more uneven, consistent with someone being escorted under pressure. This is the route Haskur was taken down.

Best for parties pursuing Haskur. The recovery route leads to the next waystation, where Haskur's transfer was processed. The party will find:

  • Where Haskur was taken.
  • Whether he is still alive at this point.
  • What “recoverable but unreliable” meant in practice.
  • A route-side perspective on Haskur's value as an asset.

Centre — The Escort Route (Kethren's Authority)

Marked by Kethren's courier mark and the seven-cuts variant. Fresh chips beside the lintel mark suggest Kethren passed through here recently — within the last week. The corridor is wider than the others, accommodating escort plus party movements.

Best for parties pursuing route culture. The escort route is what regular under-road traffic uses. The party will find:

  • How couriers like Kethren actually live on the route.
  • The route's mid-level administrative culture.
  • The first hints of what waits beyond couriers — the deeper-authority mark appears more frequently along this route.
  • The fear-of-recall pattern in route personnel.

Right — The Supply Route (Blooming Rot Logistics)

Marked by the cargo-tally mark and seed-and-wax indicators. Faint waxy smears on the floor suggest crate movement: someone wheeled cargo through here recently. This is the route Blooming Rot samples and recovered crates were taken down.

Best for parties pursuing the operation's purpose. The supply route leads to a logistics waystation specialised for cargo. The party will find:

  • Where the Blooming Rot samples were taken.
  • The full scope of the recovery infrastructure.
  • How surface bonded-claim filings paired with planned crop damage.
  • The financial machinery of the conspiracy from its route side.

The fourth, less-marked option — Return

The party can also turn back. This is a real choice. Phase 8's outcome F (Held at the Threshold) was a similar choice at the upper threshold; here, after seeing the under-road, the party may decide they have enough evidence and need to surface.

The return path is the way they came — but if the Black Ledger Gate was forced or bypassed loudly, the return is dangerous. The route's wardens at the waystation will have called for reinforcement, and the party will be facing a hostile waystation on the way back. See the Endings journal for full guidance on Outcome G (Retreat with Proof).

Handout

The party receives Handout 8 — Lower Route Marks here if they have not already received it at the Marker Gallery. Describe the three lintel marks. Do not explain where each route leads beyond the general description above.

Phase 9 ends here

The Phase 9 ending read-aloud (in the Endings journal) plays at the moment the party either:

  1. Steps into one of the three corridors, committing to a direction; or
  2. Steps back toward the gate, committing to retreat.

See the Endings journal for the closing read-aloud and the eight outcome packages.
